title: End of module: Networks and the Web

questions.text: Which of the following best describes a Wide Area Network (WAN)?
choices: A network confined to a single building or site, owned and managed by one organisation
A network that spans a large geographical area, often connecting multiple LANs, typically using third-party infrastructure
A network with no hardware devices, only cables
A network that can only ever connect exactly two computers directly
questions.explanation: A WAN spans a large geographical area and typically connects multiple LANs using infrastructure such as leased lines or satellite links — the Internet is the largest example. A LAN, by contrast, is confined to one site and owned by a single organisation.

questions.text: In a star network topology, what typically happens if the central switch fails?
choices: Only the device directly attached to the faulty cable loses its connection
Every device loses the ability to communicate, since all traffic passes through the central switch
Nothing changes — star networks have no single point of failure
The devices automatically reconnect directly to each other, forming a bus
questions.explanation: In a star topology every device's traffic passes through the central switch, so the switch is a single point of failure: if it fails, no device can communicate with any other.

questions.text: In a bus network topology, what usually happens if the single shared cable breaks?
choices: Nothing — bus networks have no single point of failure
Only the device that happened to be transmitting at that instant is affected
The whole network, or a whole section of it, can stop working, since every device shares that one cable
Data automatically re-routes through a spare central switch
questions.explanation: All devices on a bus share one physical cable, so a break in it can disrupt communication for every device on that segment — there is no central switch to route around the fault.

questions.text: Which of the following are true of packet switching?
choices: Data is broken into small units called packets before being transmitted
Every packet belonging to one message must travel via exactly the same route
Each packet contains a header (e.g. source/destination address and sequence number) plus a payload
Packets can arrive out of order and are reassembled at the destination using their sequence numbers
questions.explanation: Packets are broken from the original data, each carries a header (source/destination address, sequence number) plus a payload, and can arrive out of order to be reassembled at the destination using those sequence numbers. The false statement is the second one — different packets belonging to the same message can take different routes across the network; they are not forced onto the same path.

questions.text: What is the primary function of a network switch?
choices: To translate domain names into IP addresses
To forward data frames between devices on the same local network, using MAC addresses
To physically connect a device to network hardware and provide it with a MAC address
To route packets between different networks based on IP addresses
questions.explanation: A switch forwards frames to the correct device within a LAN based on the destination MAC address. Option 1 describes DNS, option 3 describes a NIC, and option 4 describes a router.

questions.text: What is the main function of a router?
choices: It stores and serves web pages directly to clients
It forwards packets between different networks, choosing a path based on the destination IP address
It only amplifies the electrical signal travelling along a single cable
It performs arithmetic and logic operations inside the CPU
questions.explanation: A router connects different networks together and forwards packets between them, deciding a path using the destination IP address — unlike a switch, which only forwards within one local network.

questions.text: In the peer-to-peer (P2P) model, compared with the client-server model:
choices: There is always a single central server responsible for every resource
Each computer (peer) can act as both a client and a server, sharing resources directly with other peers
Only clients may request data; peers can never provide data to one another
A P2P network cannot function unless a dedicated server manages it
questions.explanation: In a P2P network there is no dedicated central server: every peer can both request resources from, and provide resources to, other peers directly. That is precisely what distinguishes it from the client-server model.

questions.text: A file of 9000 bytes is sent using packet switching, where each packet can carry a maximum payload of 2000 bytes. How many packets are needed to send the whole file?
questions.explanation: $9000 / 2000 = 4.5$. Four full packets carry $4 \times 2000 = 8000$ bytes, leaving $1000$ bytes for a fifth, partly-filled packet — so $5$ packets are needed in total (round up, since a partial packet still requires a whole packet).

questions.text: Which statement best describes the role of TCP in the TCP/IP stack?
choices: It translates domain names into IP addresses
It splits data into segments and provides reliable delivery: acknowledgement, retransmission of lost data, and reassembly in the correct order
It defines the physical/electrical signalling used on network cables
It assigns a permanent MAC address to every network interface card
questions.explanation: TCP operates at the transport layer: it segments the application's data, tracks acknowledgements, retransmits lost or corrupted segments, and reassembles segments in the correct order at the destination.

questions.text: Which statement best describes the role of IP in the TCP/IP stack?
choices: It provides addressing of devices and routes packets across different networks
It guarantees that data arrives without errors and in the correct order
It encrypts data for secure transmission between a browser and a server
It formats the visual layout of a web page for display
questions.explanation: IP operates at the internet layer: it gives devices addresses and routes packets across interconnected networks. It does not itself guarantee ordered or error-free delivery — that reliability is provided by TCP above it.

questions.text: Which application-layer protocol is used to transfer files between a client and a server — for example, uploading a website's files to its host? Name the protocol.
questions.explanation: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) is used to transfer files between a client and a server.

questions.text: Which protocol is used to send email — from a client to its outgoing mail server, and between mail servers as a message is relayed towards its recipient? Name the protocol.
questions.explanation: SMTP (Simple Mail Transfer Protocol) is the application-layer protocol used to send email.

questions.text: What is the purpose of DNS (Domain Name System)?
choices: It encrypts data sent between a browser and a web server
It translates human-readable domain names (e.g. www.example.com) into the IP addresses computers use to locate each other
It compresses files before they are transmitted over a network
It assigns a MAC address to a network interface card at manufacture
questions.explanation: DNS acts like a phone book for the Internet, translating domain names that people can remember into the numeric IP addresses that computers actually use to route traffic.

questions.text: Which of the following statements correctly distinguish the Internet from the World Wide Web?
choices: The Internet is the underlying global network infrastructure of interconnected devices and networks
The World Wide Web is a service that runs on top of the Internet, made up of linked documents accessed via HTTP/HTTPS
The Internet and the World Wide Web are exactly the same thing, just two different names
Email (SMTP) and file transfer (FTP) are Internet services that are not part of the Web
questions.explanation: The Internet is the network infrastructure itself; the Web is one service built on top of it, made of hyperlinked documents accessed via HTTP/HTTPS. They are not the same thing, so the third statement is false — email and file transfer are examples of other Internet services that exist outside the Web, so the fourth statement is true.
